design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate nonlinear cave maze, collect required items to free trapped companions and retrieve Raj diamond, return to starting point
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity (invisible items, limited visibility into maze layout) combined with enemy attrition (three-life system with earned continues)
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Exploration and rescue adventure in vast subterranean caverns; detective-like puzzle solving through maze navigation and item collection
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deliberately obscure item placement (invisible items only grabbable at specific jump moments) combined with nonlinear progression forces player investment in maze memorization and experimentation; this friction is intentional, not incidental

difficulty.lives_system: Start with 3 lives; lose one on enemy contact or hazard; earn extra lives at 50,000 points, then 80,000 points
difficulty.continues_system: Lives earned through point accumulation serve as continue mechanism
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency in CoCo 3 port: expert mode features invisible items and standard lives; explorer mode offers unlimited lives with visible items. NES version offers no difficulty selection.

progression.upgrade_system: Temporary power-ups collected in caverns: spare pistols, invulnerability items; key items (playing card suit items) unlock rock doors; specific story items (photograph of Underground Kingdom, items to free Rhonda and Quickclaw) gate progression toward goal
progression.unlock_mechanism: Playing card suit-shaped items open rock doors; story item acquisition gates access to rescue objective and exit path
replayability_hooks: Large-scale nonlinear maze encourages different exploration routes
Hidden and secret areas reward thorough exploration
Point accumulation toward extra lives incentivizes enemy engagement
Invisible item placement creates memory challenge for repeat plays

world_building_techniques: Environmental storytelling through cave maze geography (Andes Mountains, subterranean dwelling)
Character objectives: rescue Rhonda (turned to stone) and Quickclaw (trapped lion)
Discovery-based narrative: players learn objectives through gameplay rather than exposition

danger_signals: Sluggish character movement ('floaty' controls) creates frustration during timed navigation
Invisible item placement and nonlinear maze can lead to dead-end progression if critical items missed
Poor graphical quality and repetitive music reduce engagement over extended play
environmental_storytelling: Vast subterranean cavern layout communicates isolation and scale
Enemy frog encounters and environmental hazards establish danger
Stone-turned-Rhonda visual indicator of peril within cave system

sources_used: Wikipedia: Super Pitfall - Gameplay section (item mechanics, life system, goal structure, character movement controls)
Wikipedia: Reception section (critical feedback on controls, graphics, music, and game design comparisons)
TCRF: Super Pitfall (NES) - Concept and loose port relationship to Pitfall II: Lost Caverns; unused graphics indicating design iteration
Wikipedia: Development section (Micronics as developer, port variations including CoCo 3 difficulty options)
